Unnamed: 30: ﻿https://www.hist-stmarys.org/history ; very likely this church. While the cathedral is more prominent, this historic church predates all the rest in the city, and it stands to reason that, based on that fact, the St. Mary's that we see in later years refers to this church.

﻿https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/St._Louis_Roman_Catholic_Church#:~:text=Saint%20Louis%20Roman%20Catholic%20Church,Roman%20Catholic%20Diocese%20of%20Buffalo%22. ; while St. Joseph's is more notable, and is the main church in the later almanac years of the 1860s/1870s, it appears that this is the only surviving pre-1833 church in the city. For that reason, I am going with this church as opposed to the one that appears first in the diocese later on.

﻿This church is in what is today Syracuse; however, at the time, Salina appears to have had a further reach, meaning this church would've likely been part of Salina: https://www.stjohnthebaptist-holytrinity.com/about/

﻿I believe that the coordinates for the old St. John - now a opera company(?) - is where there used to be the oldest church. As with Rochester, a lot of the old churches have been taken down, closed, or repurposed, it seems, so figuring out which one is this 1833 mention is quite difficult.
﻿Hard to identify which one - there are too many in Troy in later years

affiliated: ﻿under the direction of the Sisters of Charity
Unnamed: 30:
﻿" the service of the sisters during the cholera outbreak that afflicted the city in 1832.  The urgent needs of the sick, including care of their children, were placed above almost all other duties of the sisters.
Within two years of the outbreak, Brooklyn received two sisters who opened an asylum and female literature society.  Their residence was described as an old-fashioned farmhouse, yet the simple establishment would branch out as several other communities developed on their side of the East River over the next few decades.." (Source)
